food_name: Nasi Briyani with Fish or Prawn
aliases: Nasi Briyani with Fish or Prawn

english: Nasi Briyani with Fish or Prawn

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general biryani entry; no specific fish-or-prawn briyani entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice dish with seafood — biryani type
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated nasi briyani fish/prawn code. A0FJP (rice-based mixed dish) is the closest defensible anchor. No code invented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for nasi briyani with fish or prawn as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of nasi briyani with fish or prawn is approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 200 g of spiced rice and 150 g of fish or prawn with gravy, as typically served at Singapore hawker centres and Indian restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Briyani (Fish or Prawn) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 with review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for Nasi Briyani (Fish or Prawn), CR ID F05100080. Saturated fat is estimated from HPB comparable briyani entries and USDA SR Legacy basmati rice with ghee component data, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — basmati rice cooked, analogous estimate for spiced basmati rice dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean nasi briyani with fish or prawn. GI of 63 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for basmati rice (cooked), which consistently returns GI values of 57–69 (mean ~63) in peer-reviewed testing. The fish/prawn protein component and ghee fat content are expected to modestly lower the effective GI relative to plain basmati rice; however, the basmati rice GI of 63 is retained as a conservative estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (63 × 61.4) / 100 ≈ 38.7, rounded to 38.8, based on 350 g serving with 17.54 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 417 mg per 100 g exceeds the 400 mg per 100 g threshold with minimal potassium or fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 200 g per meal, request reduced-salt preparation, and avoid additional high-sodium condiments such as sambal or pickled achar, as a standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 1460 mg sodium — more than 60% of the 2400 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate density (17.5 g per 100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.16 g per 100 g) and medium GI of 63 from basmati rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit serving to approximately 150–200 g, pair with a high-fibre vegetable side to slow glucose absorption, and prioritise the fish or prawn protein component to improve the protein-to-carbohydrate ratio and moderate postprandial glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (417 mg per 100 g) and saturated fat from ghee (~2.1 g per 100 g) are the primary cardiovascular risk factors; fish or prawn protein provides partial offset via omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request preparation with minimal ghee or substitute with a plant-based oil, choose fish over prawn to maximise omega-3 benefit, and keep total serving to 250 g or less to limit sodium and saturated fat intake within cardiovascular dietary targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spice blend (turmeric, ginger, cinnamon, cloves) and omega-3 fatty acids from fish provide joint-protective benefit
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Nasi briyani with fish is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to its anti-inflammatory spice profile and omega-3 content; patients with nightshade sensitivity should monitor response to tomato in the gravy, and ghee should be minimised to reduce saturated fat load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Briyani with Fish or Prawn is a fragrant, spiced rice dish that is a staple of Singapore's Indian-Muslim hawker cuisine. The dish is prepared by cooking long-grain basmati rice in ghee (clarified butter) together with a complex spice blend that typically includes turmeric, cumin, coriander, cinnamon, cardamom, cloves, and star anise, producing a golden-coloured, aromatic rice with a rich, buttery flavour and slightly firm texture. The rice is served alongside or layered with a portion of fish (commonly ikan kurau, red snapper, or mackerel) or prawns cooked in a spiced tomato-onion-yogurt gravy. The protein component adds a savoury, mildly spicy depth to the dish. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 147 kcal, 6.61 g protein, 5.55 g fat, and 17.54 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 417 mg — notably elevated due to salt in the spice marinade and gravy. Dietary fibre is low at 1.16 g per 100 g. The basmati rice base carries a medium glycemic index of approximately 63, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The anti-inflammatory spice blend, particularly turmeric (curcumin) and ginger, confers potential benefit for inflammatory conditions. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore hawker settings and is a common working lunch and festive meal choice across Indian, Malay, and broader Singaporean communities.

Is nasi briyani suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat nasi briyani if I have high blood pressure?

What is the difference between fish briyani and prawn briyani?
Is nasi briyani good for weight loss?
What spices are in nasi briyani?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database (CR ID F05100080) with review_status reviewed and confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for basmati rice, which is the primary carbohydrate component. Saturated fat is estimated from HPB comparable briyani entries and USDA SR Legacy component data. Overall record confidence is high given the exact HPB match for the primary macros.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.1 g per 100 g) is estimated from HPB comparable briyani dish entries and USDA SR Legacy data for basmati rice cooked with ghee; HPB does not separately publish saturated fat for this specific item. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid fractionation data for this specific Singapore hawker briyani preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. GI of 63 is an analogous estimate based on basmati rice (cooked) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Singaporean nasi briyani with fish or prawn has been published in peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. 4. Glycemic load per serving is calculated as (63 × 61.4 g carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 ≈ 38.8. 5. The dish may be prepared with either fish or prawn; the nutrient profile reflects the HPB composite entry and individual variation will occur depending on protein choice and preparation method. 6. FoodEx2 code A0FJP is the closest defensible anchor for a rice-based mixed dish; no dedicated nasi briyani fish/prawn code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2026.
